When the plant burns, its urushiol oil rides along on the smoke particles, and breathing that in can trigger a reaction not just on the skin but inside the airways and lungs. People have been hospitalized after inhaling poison-ivy smoke from a brush fire or campfire. The plant that gives a nasty itch on contact becomes genuinely dangerous when it goes up in flames.
summary: Burning poison ivy sends urushiol into the smoke, which can cause a dangerous internal reaction if inhaled.

headline: Climate Change Is Making Poison Ivy Worse
body: Poison ivy may be one of the winners of a warming world, and not in a way people will like. Experiments that raise carbon-dioxide levels to those expected in coming decades find that poison ivy grows faster and larger, and, crucially, produces a more potent, itchier form of its urushiol oil. As CO2 in the atmosphere climbs, researchers expect bigger, nastier poison ivy spreading more aggressively through forests and backyards.
summary: Higher CO2 makes poison ivy grow bigger and produce a more potent, itchier form of its rash oil.

headline: Wild Venus Flytraps Grow in Only One Small Area
body: The Venus flytrap is famous around the world, yet in the wild it grows naturally in just one tiny place: a roughly 75-mile radius of boggy pine savanna around Wilmington, North Carolina. Nowhere else on Earth does it occur natively. Because that home is so small and the plants are so coveted, poaching became a real threat, and in North Carolina digging up wild Venus flytraps is now a felony crime.
summary: Wild Venus flytraps grow only within about 75 miles of Wilmington, NC, where poaching them is a felony.

headline: A Venus Flytrap Doesn't Eat Bugs for Food
body: It is easy to assume a Venus flytrap survives by devouring insects, but that is not how it makes a living. Like other green plants, it powers itself through photosynthesis, turning sunlight into energy. So why the traps? It grows in boggy soil so poor in nutrients that it supplements its diet by catching bugs, mainly to harvest the nitrogen locked inside them. The insects are less like food and more like a vitamin packet or fertilizer.
summary: A Venus flytrap lives on sunlight; it catches bugs mainly for nitrogen its poor soil lacks.

headline: A Venus Flytrap Can Actually Count
body: The Venus flytrap does simple math to avoid wasting energy on false alarms. Tiny trigger hairs line each trap, and a single touch, which might just be a raindrop or a blown leaf, does nothing. Only if a second touch comes within about 20 seconds does the trap snap shut. Then it keeps counting: it takes around five triggers, as the struggling prey thrashes, before the plant commits to producing digestive juices. It literally counts to survive.
summary: A Venus flytrap counts touches, snapping shut only after two and digesting only after about five.

headline: Washington's First Cherry Trees Had to Be Burned
body: Washington, D.C.'s celebrated cherry trees almost never happened. Japan's first gift, about 2,000 trees shipped in 1910, arrived infested with insects and disease, and American officials made the painful decision to burn the entire shipment to protect local plants. Rather than take offense, Japan responded with extraordinary grace, sending an even larger and healthier gift of over 3,000 trees in 1912. Those are the ancestors of the blossoms crowds flock to today.
summary: Japan's first 2,000 cherry trees arrived diseased and were burned, so Japan sent 3,000 more in 1912.

headline: Cherry Records Span 1,200 Years and Reveal Warming
body: In Japan, people have logged the peak bloom of cherry blossoms for an astonishingly long time. Records from Kyoto stretch back more than 1,200 years, one of the longest running seasonal datasets in the world. That deep archive has become accidental climate science. The blossoms are now peaking earlier than at almost any point in that vast span, a clear sign of warming, written in the timing of falling petals across twelve centuries.
summary: Kyoto's 1,200-year record of cherry blooms now shows the earliest peaks ever, evidence of warming.

headline: Cherry Blossoms Are Loved Because They Barely Last
body: A cherry tree's spectacular bloom is heartbreakingly brief, often lasting only about a week before the petals let go and drift down like snow. In Japan, that fleeting quality is the whole point. The blossoms are the great symbol of mono no aware, a bittersweet awareness of how beautiful things are precisely because they do not last. Crowds gather for hanami, flower viewing, to sit beneath the trees and savor a beauty they know is already ending.
summary: Cherry blossoms last only about a week, and that fleeting beauty is central to Japan's hanami tradition.

headline: The Biggest Living Thing Grew From an Oat-Sized Seed
body: The giant sequoia named General Sherman is the largest single living thing on Earth by volume, a trunk so massive it weighs more than ten blue whales combined and has stood for over 2,000 years. The almost unbelievable part is where it started. A giant sequoia seed is roughly the size of a flake of oatmeal, so light that about 91,000 of them weigh just one pound. Earth's most colossal organism begins as a near-weightless speck.
summary: General Sherman, the biggest living thing, outweighs ten blue whales but grew from an oat-flake seed.

headline: Giant Sequoias Actually Need Fire to Survive
body: For a long time people assumed protecting sequoia forests meant stopping every fire. It turned out that was quietly killing them. Giant sequoias depend on fire to reproduce. The heat dries and pops open their tightly sealed cones, releasing seeds, and it clears away competing plants and litter to expose the bare, mineral soil the seeds need to sprout. Decades of aggressive fire suppression left many groves unable to raise new sequoias at all.
summary: Giant sequoias need fire, whose heat opens their cones and clears ground for seeds, so fire suppression harmed them.

headline: People Once Drove Cars Through a Living Sequoia
body: In an era eager to show off nature's giants, a tunnel was carved straight through the trunk of a living giant sequoia in California's Yosemite region in the 1880s. Wide enough for stagecoaches and later automobiles, the Wawona Tunnel Tree became a wildly popular attraction, with tourists posing as they drove through a tree. It stood, hole and all, for decades until a heavy snow load finally toppled the weakened giant in 1969.
summary: A tunnel cut through a living Yosemite sequoia in the 1880s let cars drive through it until it fell in 1969.

headline: The 'World's Biggest Flower' Is Not Really a Flower
body: The towering corpse flower is often crowned the world's biggest flower, but that title is a botanical mix-up. What rises up to about 10 feet is not a single bloom at all; it is a giant spike ringed at its base by thousands of tiny individual flowers. The true record for the largest single flower belongs to a different plant entirely, the parasitic Rafflesia of Southeast Asia, which grows a single bloom up to three feet across, and also reeks of rot.
summary: The corpse flower is a spike of thousands of tiny flowers; the real biggest single bloom is the Rafflesia.

headline: The Corpse Flower Heats Itself to Spread Its Stink
body: The corpse flower does not just passively smell bad; it actively works to broadcast its stench of rotting flesh. As it blooms, the tall central spike warms itself up to around human body temperature, a rare feat for a plant. That self-generated heat helps vaporize its foul odor and lift it into the air, carrying the smell of decay as far as half a mile to summon the carrion beetles and flesh flies that pollinate it.
summary: A blooming corpse flower heats its spike to near body temperature to waft its rotting-flesh stench half a mile.

headline: A Corpse Flower Bloom Draws Crowds and Livestreams
body: A corpse flower blooming is a genuine event, because it happens so rarely and so briefly. A plant may go seven to ten years between flowerings, and when it finally opens, the spectacle lasts barely a day or two before it collapses. So when a botanical garden's corpse flower is about to bloom, thousands of people line up for hours, sometimes overnight, to catch a whiff of its infamous stench, and many gardens set up round-the-clock webcams to livestream it.
summary: Corpse flowers bloom only every 7 to 10 years for barely a day, so crowds line up and gardens livestream it.

headline: Mangroves Give Birth to Live Seedlings
body: Most trees drop seeds and hope for the best, but many mangroves do something closer to live birth. Their seeds germinate while still attached to the parent tree, growing into long, javelin-shaped seedlings called propagules. When ready, these drop straight down and can spear point-first into the soft mud below, instantly rooted. If they land in water instead, they can float for months, drifting on the tides until they wash up on a distant shore.
summary: Many mangroves sprout live, spear-shaped seedlings on the parent that drop into the mud or float away.

headline: Some Mangroves Breathe Through Snorkel Roots
body: Mangroves grow in waterlogged coastal mud that is almost completely starved of oxygen, which would suffocate the roots of an ordinary tree. Their solution is to breathe through snorkels. Many species send up pneumatophores, finger-like roots that poke straight up out of the mud into the air. Dotted with tiny pores, these vertical roots draw in oxygen and pipe it down to the buried root system, letting the tree breathe even at high tide.
summary: Many mangroves grow vertical 'snorkel' roots that poke out of the airless mud to breathe.

headline: A Wall of Mangroves Can Blunt a Tsunami
body: Mangrove forests are one of the best natural defenses a coastline can have. Their dense thicket of tangled roots and trunks absorbs the energy of waves, wind, and surging water, holding shorelines together instead of letting them wash away. After the devastating 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, studies found that coastal villages sheltered behind thick mangroves generally suffered far less death and destruction than those on stripped, unprotected shores.
summary: Mangroves' dense roots absorb wave energy, and villages behind them fared far better in the 2004 tsunami.

headline: A Bonsai Is Not a Special Dwarf Tree
body: A common myth is that bonsai are some genetically miniature species of tree. They are not. A bonsai can be almost any ordinary tree, an oak, a pine, a maple, that would grow to full size in the ground. It stays tiny only through relentless human intervention: careful pruning of its branches and, crucially, its roots, plus confinement to a shallow pot. Left alone, a bonsai would simply start growing into a normal, full-sized tree.
summary: Bonsai are ordinary tree species kept miniature purely by pruning roots and branches, not by genetics.

headline: Some Bonsai Are Centuries Old and Worth Millions
body: Because a bonsai can theoretically live as long as any tree, the finest ones are living heirlooms handed down for generations. Some prized specimens are several hundred years old, patiently shaped by a lineage of caretakers. That history and artistry can carry a staggering price. Exceptional old bonsai have reportedly sold for over a million dollars, making a small tree in a pot one of the most valuable living artworks a person can own.
summary: Top bonsai are passed down for centuries, and exceptional specimens have sold for over a million dollars.

headline: A Bonsai in Washington Survived Hiroshima
body: Among the bonsai Japan gave the United States for its 1976 bicentennial was a white pine already about 400 years old. For decades no one at the museum knew its secret. Only in 2001, when the donor's family visited, was the astonishing truth revealed: the tree had been growing at a nursery in Hiroshima, roughly two miles from where the atomic bomb detonated in 1945. It survived the blast, and its keepers had said nothing for 25 years.
summary: A 400-year-old bonsai gifted to the US survived the Hiroshima bombing two miles away, unknown until 2001.
